messages.content:
What was Augustus' relationship to Julius Caesar?

All of the above.


He was Caesar's grandnephew.

 

He was Caesar's successor.


He was Caesar's heir.

All of the above statements are correct. Augustus was Caesar's grandnephew, as his great-nephew through his biological father. He was also Caesar's successor, as he was appointed as his adopted son and heir.

Octavian added Imperator as part of his name. In doing so he was telling the world that:

He was a dictator for life.


He was the general in charge of all of Rome's armies.

 

All Roman military victories were a result of his leadership.


Roman military power extended all the way to Egypt.

The statement "He was the general in charge of all of Rome's armies" is the most accurate interpretation of Octavian adding Imperator to his name. It signified that he had supreme command and control over the Roman military forces. The other statements are either incorrect or oversimplified. Octavian's claim to power and his title of Imperator were not synonymous with being a dictator for life, and it did not directly imply that all Roman military victories were solely due to his leadership. Additionally, Roman military power extended beyond Egypt during Octavian's time, so that statement is not entirely accurate either.

Which of the following names was used by the first emperor of Rome?

Imperator


Caesar


Augustus

 

All of the above


Octavian

All of the above names were used by the first emperor of Rome. The first emperor of Rome, commonly known as Augustus, used the titles Imperator, Caesar, and Augustus. Octavian was his original name before he became the emperor.

The proscriptions of the second triumvirate placed the members of the triumvirate in positions of incredible wealth and power. Their power over the senate is evidenced by Octavian:

Ridding the senate of the disgruntled consul members.


Being named Imperator, a member of the Roman consul.

 

Being named Marc Antony, a member of the Second Triumvirate


Being named Divi Filius, the son of a god

The statement "Ridding the senate of the disgruntled consul members" is the most accurate in relation to Octavian's power over the senate. As a member of the Second Triumvirate, Octavian, along with Marc Antony and Lepidus, had the authority to purify or purge the senate by removing or executing those they perceived as threats or opponents. While Octavian was named Imperator and held the position of consul in the Roman government, it is not directly related to his power over the senate. Similarly, being named Marc Antony and Divi Filius (son of a god) were not factors in Octavian's control over the senate.

The second triumvirate was formed in response to:

The Roman Senate joining forces with Brutus and other fugitive senators in hopes of ousting Octavian.


Octavian losing the Battle of Gaul to Mark Antony.

 

The Roman senate allowing Octavian to have legal control over the armies of Rome.


Octavian beating Marc Antony at the Battle of Gaul.

The second triumvirate was formed in response to the Roman Senate joining forces with Brutus and other fugitive senators in hopes of ousting Octavian. This alliance between the Roman Senate and the conspirators involved in the assassination of Julius Caesar led Octavian, along with Mark Antony and Lepidus, to form the second triumvirate as a means to consolidate their power and eliminate their enemies. The other options provided are not accurate in describing the reasons for the formation of the second triumvirate.

During the Julio-Claudian Dynasty, how was Claudius able to become emperor?

He was the son of the previous emperor, Caligula.


He won the position in a battle with Caligula.

 

He was elected by the senate.


He was selected by the army.

During the Julio-Claudian Dynasty, Claudius was able to become the emperor because he was selected by the army. After the assassination of Caligula, the Praetorian Guard, the imperial bodyguard, declared Claudius as the new emperor. He was not the son of the previous emperor, Caligula, and he did not win the position in a battle with Caligula. Additionally, he was not elected by the senate, but rather chosen by the military faction.

Which event contributed the most to Tiberius' eventual execution by his own guard?

He lost political power to his advisor, Sejanus.


He refused to leave the island of Capri.

 

His increased paranoia resulted in multiple executions.


He lacked interest in the pomp of being an emperor.

The statement "His increased paranoia resulted in multiple executions" is the event that contributed the most to Tiberius' eventual execution by his own guard. Tiberius became increasingly paranoid in his later years, leading to a series of purges and executions of perceived threats or potential rivals. This behavior eroded his support and led to resentment among his own guard, ultimately contributing to his downfall. The other options do not directly relate to Tiberius' execution by his own guard.

Why was Octavian's name changed to Augustus?

Because that is always the name given to the first emperor of a new dynasty.


Because Augustus also meant First Citizen.

 

To mark him as a great and revered leader after he beat Mark Antony in battle.


To differentiate him from Emperor Octavian II, whom he succeeded.

Octavian's name was changed to Augustus for several reasons. One reason is that Augustus also meant "First Citizen," which conveyed the idea of a republic and emphasized his position as the leader of Rome while maintaining the illusion of traditional Roman government structures. Another reason was to mark him as a great and revered leader after his victory over Mark Antony in battle, solidifying his position as the sole ruler of Rome. The option mentioning Emperor Octavian II is incorrect, as Octavian took the name Augustus during his own reign and was not succeeded by an Emperor Octavian II.

Which emperor succeeded an emperor that was his nephew?

Caligula


Augustus

 

Claudius


Tiberius

The emperor who succeeded an emperor that was his nephew was Tiberius. Tiberius succeeded Augustus, who was his stepfather and the great-uncle of Tiberius. Caligula was the successor of Tiberius, Claudius succeeded Caligula, and there is no emperor mentioned after Tiberius, so the correct answer is Tiberius.
